<!-- Topic 4: Arrays and Pointers -->
<!-- Slides 43–60 -->

# Arrays and Pointers
<!-- Slide 43 -->

## Have We Already Used Pointers? {.smaller}

- C-style arrays already expose address-based behavior
- Pointers explain why familiar array syntax works

::: notes
Slides 43–60
:::

<!-- Slide 44 -->

---

## Array Elements Occupy Contiguous Memory

```{.cpp}
int values[4] = {12, 18, 25, 31};
```

| Element | `values[0]` | `values[1]` | `values[2]` | `values[3]` |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Value | 12 | 18 | 25 | 31 |
| Offset | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |

<!-- Slide 45 -->

---

## Every Element Has an Address

```{.cpp}
int values[4] = {12, 18, 25, 31};

for (int index = 0; index < 4; index++) {
    cout << values[index] << " at "
         << &values[index] << "\n";
}
```

<!-- Slide 46 -->

---

## An Array Name Identifies Its First Element

```{.cpp}
int values[4] = {12, 18, 25, 31};

cout << values << "\n";
cout << &values[0] << "\n";
```

- In most expressions, the array name converts to a pointer to its first element

<!-- Slide 47 -->

---

## Store the First Element's Address

```{.cpp}
int values[4] = {12, 18, 25, 31};
int* ptr = values;

cout << ptr << "\n";
cout << *ptr << "\n"; // 12
```

<!-- Slide 48 -->

---

## Indexing Is Indirect Access

```{.cpp}
cout << values[0] << "\n";
cout << *values << "\n";
```

`values[0] == *(values + 0)`

<!-- Slide 49 -->

---

## Indexes Translate Into Address Offsets

```{.cpp}
for (int index = 0; index < 4; index++) {
    cout << values[index] << " "
         << *(values + index) << "\n";
}
```

`values[index] == *(values + index)`

<!-- Slide 50 -->

---

## Pointers Can Use Subscripts

```{.cpp}
int* ptr = values;

cout << values[2] << "\n";
cout << ptr[2] << "\n";
cout << *(ptr + 2) << "\n";
```

<!-- Slide 51 -->

---

## Two Ways to Read an Array

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{.cpp}
for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
    cout << values[i] << "\n";
}
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{.cpp}
for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
    cout << *(values + i) << "\n";
}
```
:::
:::

<!-- Slide 52 -->

---

## Two Ways to Modify an Array

```{.cpp}
int values[4] = {12, 18, 25, 31};

for (int index = 0; index < 4; index++) {
    *(values + index) *= 2;
}

for (int value : values) {
    cout << value << " ";
}
```

<!-- Slide 53 -->

---

## Start in the Middle

```{.cpp}
int values[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
int* ptr = &values[2];

for (int offset = 0; offset < 3; offset++) {
    cout << ptr[offset] << "\n";
}
```

<!-- Slide 54 -->

---

## The Pointer Can Walk; the Array Name Cannot

```{.cpp}
int values[3] = {7, 8, 9};
int* ptr = values;

ptr++;
cout << *ptr << "\n"; // 8

// values++; // compiler error
```

<!-- Slide 55 -->

---

## Arrays Become Pointers at Function Boundaries

```{.cpp}
void display(const int values[], int size);
void display(const int* values, int size);
```

- For a parameter, these declarations describe the same interface
- The function receives the first-element address, not the whole array object

<!-- Slide 56 -->

---

## The Function Still Needs the Size

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int sum(const int values[], int size);

int main() {
    int values[4] = {2, 4, 6, 8};
    cout << sum(values, 4) << "\n";
    return 0;
}

int sum(const int values[], int size) {
    int total = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) total += values[i];
    return total;
}
```

<!-- Slide 57 -->

---

## No Copy Required

```{.cpp}
int values[6] = {5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30};
int* beginning = &values[2];
int length = 3;
int total = 0;

for (int i = 0; i < length; i++) {
    total += beginning[i];
}

cout << total << "\n"; // 60
```

<!-- Slide 58 -->

---

## An Array and a Pointer Are Related, Not Identical

```{.cpp}
int values[5] = {};
int* ptr = values;

cout << sizeof(values) << "\n";
cout << sizeof(ptr) << "\n";
```

- The array owns storage for five integers
- The pointer stores one address and can be reseated

<!-- Slide 59 -->

---

## Pointers Explain Familiar Array Behavior

- Array names usually convert to first-element pointers
- Subscripting performs indirect access with an offset
- An address plus a length can describe any contiguous subrange

<!-- Slide 60 -->
